desc:
The gently flowing stream makes a sudden turn to the south and increases its
speed as it hits the steep face of a rocky bank. Impressive stone walls of a
stronghold rest high upon the steep river bank to the west. Far to the south a
large forest can be seen beyond the river.
name: Bend in the Stream

desc:
A large rocky outcrop in the steep river banks forces the stream to bend here,
creating much turbulence. The water is icy cold and fresh, and tastes like the
crisp snow from which it melted. Fish beneath you can be seen struggling with
the currents, many just managing to remain in the same spot without any headway.
To the south a ridge climbs up into the forest, along which the river flows for
many miles westward.
name: Bend in the Stream

desc:
The waters become troubled and turbulent here, with some rocks jutting out from
the streambed creating powerful flows and eddies. Salmon can be seen leaping
over natural weirs and waterfalls, in an effort to return to their spawning
grounds. From the north bank a pier protrudes out of a small village, ten feet
into the river.
name: Fast Flowing Stream

desc:
A wide pool of water with small mounds of sticks and branches dominates your
view, with the trees sloping down to banks on each side. On the southern bank
the trees extend out into the water, where the beaver's dam has caused the
stream to burst its banks. Many of the small creatures can be seen scurrying
about, along the dam and on the piles of wood in the water. The dam blocks
further travel downstream, unless you clamber onto the banks to walk around it.
name: Behind the Dam

desc:
A small dam blocks passage further upstream from here, beavers scurrying about
its top. The dam is made from a variety of small sticks and branches, carefully
managed into place by these industrious little creatures. Southwards the trees
dip down to the stream, making progress in that direction possible, although the
land looks quite marshy there. Westwards a stand of great willows can be seen,
overhanging the river.
name: Beaver Dam

desc:
A grove of willows gently bends over the stream, almost providing a roof above
you. Boats of leaves float gently on the water, intermingled with the floating
branches. The beds of reeds on the southern bank seem to be a hive of activity,
providing a cacophony of sound. Behind the reeds the forest climbs up the very
steep bank. Eastwards a dam can be seen clearly, a construction of countless
branches and mulched leaves. Westwards the stream continues drifting along,
downstream towards the sea.
name: Tunnel of Trees

desc:
Floating logs gently bump and push you around, making it difficult to move in
any direction. The river is full of wildlife - frogs can be seen sitting on the
logs, croaking away looking for a mate. Other wildlife can be seen walking on
the banks, or diving into the river. Fish can be seen idly swimming underneath
you, carrying on with their lives oblivious to your presence. A small dam made
from sticks is visible upstream from here.
name: Amid Floating Logs

desc:
Flies and other water insects buzz over the river. Its flat calm surface is only
interrupted by the occasional fish, giving off ripples that echo across the
water. Every now and again a log or branch drifts past you on its voyage
downstream. The river itself marks the boundary between plains and the woods,
perched above you on a steep slope.
name: Drifting by the Forest

desc:
The stream is a slow calm flow at this point, with the water clear enough to see
the bottom a few feet away. Small fish can be seen darting under sunken timbers.
The stream can be seen upriver, nearer to its mountain source, and westwards,
where it lessens to trickling shallows. A trail fords the river there,
continuing north into the plains and south into the woods.
name: Forest Stream

desc:
The trail crosses a small stream here, the water shallow but very cold. The
stream runs westward, away from its mountain source, its headway prevented by a
small weir just west of here. On the south bank the forest continues, with a
long overgrown trail barely visible. Constant woodland chirpings emanate from
the trees, with fleeting glances at local fauna the only clue to their source.
name: A Shallow Ford

desc:
The worn path makes a long turn from west to north along a high, dark stone wall
with small loopholes and stones projecting from its coping. The once partially
cultivated ground is now overtaken by wild grass and small bushes giving it a
deserted look.
name: Path by the Wall

desc:
The worn path forks in three as it approaches the wooden stakes of what looks
like a village. One of the branches of the path heads straight east towards a
large open area in front of the gate, while another travels north across a
natural bridge over a fissure. To the west grasslands take hold, stretching far
into the distance.
name: Three Way Crossing

desc:
A worn path ends in a large flat area in front of a gate. Behind the clinging
vines that grow on it, a glimpse of a small village can be seen. To the north a
deep fissure breaks the line of the plains, but soon after the fissure the
plains continue to dominate the landscape.
name: By the Gate

desc:
The grassy field changes into a muddy slope covered with ferns and osier as it
gets closer to the stream. Large willows lines the stream where the ground is
less damp. Some of the great trees have been cut down leaving only the stump to
moulder away. North a deep crack in the earth blocks any passage.
name: Muddy Riverside

desc:
A small path leading through a once partly cultivated field makes a sudden turn
by a small clump of rowans, heading down a slight slope to the south. Further
down the slope, the ground is covered by ferns and brushwood where the sounds of
water can be heard, streaming across the wide ford.
name: Well Worn Path

desc:
The plains are covered with short, dry grass and gently slopes downwards to the
south. In some spots, flat rocks lay bare due to the influence of erosion on the
land. To the east the ground climbs abruptly down into a steep ridge, making
travel difficult.
name: Sloping Grassland

desc:
Dry, billowing grass makes a rustling sound as the wind catches it for as far as
the eye can see. The lack of anything but plains makes this area quiet and
desolate. A large boulder is here providing shelter from the wind that rushes
through the plains off the Misty Mountains.
name: Desolate Plains

desc:
A large depression cuts deep into the billowing grasslands where the view over
the surrounding plains is blocked on all sides by the high edge. From here only
the face of the depression and the sky are able to be seen. The bottom of the
depression is covered with large rocks, and the ground is like clay between the
boulders. It is possible to move north from here up into the plains, and west
into a deep fissure.
name: The Depression

desc:
Cut clean into the plains like an axe mark on a tree, this crevice is deep and
narrow. At its base the light is weak, and movement tricky due to the countless
rocks littering the ground. Eastwards the crevice gives way to a depression in
the plains, and easier ground. West the light is too weak to distinguish any
features.
name: Crevice in the Plains

desc:
Above you the sides of the fissure join and form a natural bridge several dozen
feet wide. Here, well below the level of the plains, light does not reach, and
it is dank and tepid. Debris, fallen from above, is strewn about the ground,
requiring some effort to clamber over. The fissure lightens to the east and west
slightly, but still the towering rock walls loom above.
name: Dark Depths of the Fissure

desc:
Not much more than twenty feet wide, the fissure in the plains runs for many
miles. Here, some hundred feet down, the light is very weak and it is easy to
stumble over the countless pieces of fallen rock. West the fissure opens up
slightly, while east it darkens further. A strong breeze whistles through here
constantly, making it hard to hear anything.
name: Bottom of a Fissure

desc:
A depression forms here, well below the level of the plains. Little grows at its
base, except for the occasional hardy shrub or thistle. South the valley towers
unclimbable above you, while north the slope climbs gently enough to move with
little difficulty. West is a more gentle route up to the flatlands above, while
the valley narrows and becomes a fissure to the east.
name: Base of a Valley

desc:
Vast and mundane grasslands, unsheltered from wind and rain, extend in every
direction but east, where the earth gently drops down into a valley where only a
few small bushes have managed to get a foothold on the barren land. Close to a
large rock lie the scattered, charred remains of a campfire, used fairly
recently. Northwards a hill lies in the plains, where a gloomy cave can be seen
on its steepest side.
name: Vast Grasslands

desc:
A steep ridge in the west breaks the monotony of the grassy fields that are
covered with high, billowing grass. The surroundings make the ridge look rather
majestic as it climbs high into the sky. Running along the edge of the ridge is
a small glittering brook lined with ferns.
name: Brook through the Grasslands

desc:
A very steep ridge running in a curve from east to south creates a windy
turbulence as the wind whirls up off the face of the ridge. Small rocks are
scattered in the high grass and the lack of vegetation keeps the surroundings
barren.
name: Plains by the Ridge

desc:
The rocky ground and the constant wind make the plains rather arid and bare on
the higher ground, with grass growing reluctantly elsewhere. A poor area for the
local wild life, but it seems they have adapted as the area is filled with many
small creatures. North the land drops into a sheer crack in the earth, while
southwards a trail runs from west to east.
name: Rolling Grasslands

desc:
The trail continues uninterrupted through the plains from the east to the west.
Only scattered rocks and sparsly growing brushwood prevent the worn route from
making a perfectly straight line through the plains. The ground gently slopes
downwards to the south where a glittering river runs at the edge of a luxurious
green forest.
name: Worn Path

desc:
A small trail stretches through the vast plains in an east-westerly direction.
Small rocks and hollows makes the trail twist and turn in an unpredictable way
across the grass covered plains. A gentle slope to the south ends in a clump of
densly packed willow trees where the sound of streaming water can be heard.
name: Worn Path through the Fields

desc:
A small worn trail winds through the damp plains covered with brushwood and
osier. The vegetation makes the trail hard to access on foot and in some places
a new path has been worn to avoid the roughest parts of the terrain. A large
stack of recently cut logs lies on the bank of a river to the south.
name: Worn Path by the River

desc:
A worn trail winds through plains covered with osier and brushwood. Parts of the
ground are particulary damp, making the clay path hard to access. The way to the
south is hampered by some large, tightly packed willows, with the sound of
rushing water prominent beyond.
name: Riverside Path

desc:
The remains of an old farm left in ruins for years lies here, divided in two by
a brook. A stone bridge that once linked the two parcels of land still stands
even now, long after the farm has fallen into disuse. The small brook flows past
the farmyard and under the bridge, out into a larger river to the south. A small
worn path, that shows signs of recent use, runs west to east through the middle
of the farmyard.
name: Small Stone Bridge over the Brook

desc:
A small brook murmurs as it twists and turns out of the east into the small
rocks and boulders that are scattered over the grassy plains. A steep ridge
blocks passabe to the west and north, forcing the brook to take a turn to the
south.
name: Brook by the Ridge

desc:
A winding brook runs west, cutting through the flat grasslands. The water cuts
deeper into the humus and is stagnant, bringing a scent of mire to the air.
Eastwards the plains are verdant, full of teeming wildlife and billowing grass,
while a trail travels across the land to the south.
name: Brook through the Grasslands

desc:
High, billowing grass covers the monotonous field that extends through the
plain. The only other vegetation that has managed to get a foothold on the
unsheltered flat are a few windswept bushes struggeling to survive. A path lies
south of here, while the plains continue to grow in all other directions.
name: Vast Grasslands

desc:
The everlasting plain is particulary rocky in this area, making travel risky and
tiring. The only vegetation that manages to grow here is high grass that looks
surprisingly lush for such a barren place like this. A path lies south of here,
and grasslands continue in all other directions.
name: Vast Grasslands

desc:
Various shrubs and bushes almost cover the trail at this point, but a path can
still be inferred from the line in the trees. Two large oaks dominate the view
eastwards, strangely solitary in the forest. Still, there does seem to be a gap
large enough for a man or animal to squeeze through. Peering between the gap,
trees stretch off into the distance. The trail continues south and the sound of
water can be heard from the north.
name: An Overgrown Trail

desc:
Moving between these two huge oak trees, ancient beyond compare, isn't easy due
to the thick undergrowth. To the west the forest thins out some and the calls of
wildlife can be heard. The sound of a river bubble away to the north, but a
sheer cliff prevents access to it. A trail cuts through the forest to the south,
with a large fallen tree in the way.
name: Between Two Large Oaks

desc:
The undergrowth is fairly sparse here, making progress a bit of a trial. Bright
flowers form a carpet on the forest floor. and the sounds of the forest seem
oblivious to your presence. A small anthill lies at the base of one of these
majestic trees, the tiny creatures carrying on their daily scurrying without
knowing or caring of the world outside their own. The trees continue to the
east, while west two large oaks almost block the way. The river is visible
below, although the bank is far too steep to descend down, and south the bushes
grow so closely movement there would be impossible.
name: Wandering in the Forest

desc:
Forest flowers provide a colorful carpet for the forest floor here, overlooking
a river a long way down. The scent coming from the flowers is all pervasive,
near intoxicating in its intensity. The trees here are fairly sparse, making
progress through the forest not particularly strenuous. To the south the trees
are covered in ivy and other climbing vines, and the undergrowth grows thicker
there.
name: Wandering in the Forest

desc:
The ground becomes soft and spongy underfoot, with a green moss covering the
forest floor. Due to the boggy ground the trees are smaller here, maybe only six
foot in height at the most. The moss when squeezed gives off a trickle of murky
water, which runs down your thumb. The lay of the land rises to the west and the
forest dries out, while eastwards it becomes even more waterlogged and marshy.
name: On Soft Footing

desc:
The stream to the north has burst its banks, flooding the forest here. The
ground has become so waterlogged that only a few trees can actually manage to
grow to any height, the result being a natural split in the forest. The forest
to the west looks slightly drier, though to the east the near marshland is
continued. The area next to the stream has been trampled into mud by animals
coming down to the stream for a drink, almost two feet of sticky bog to sink
into.
name: Boggy Split

desc:
The stream has fully burst its banks here and flooded the forest here, due to
the beaver dam blocking it. A bank in the east lifts the forest away out of the
water, while the trees get quite boggy to the west. Dense forest to the south
prevents you from getting out of this water, full of fish darting between the
underwater tree trunks.
name: Watery Forest

desc:
Drooping branches of the willow trees give some good shelter here. The grass
underneath is lush and green, and makes a good resting spot. The forest begins
to ascend a ridge to the south, passable but steep, but to the north the steep
drop blocks access to the river. Eastwards dozens of yards of briars provide an
effective barrier.
name: In a Grove of Willows

desc:
A small clearing opens in this glade, lined with countless small delicate
flowers. A small column of wispy white smoke rises up through the trees to the
east, soon disappearing into the sky. Tightly packed trees and briars form a
complete barrier to the west, while the steep cliff northwards looks impossible
to climb down. At its base runs a river, following the line of the ridge for
many miles.
name: In a Small Clearing

desc:
The ground here rises to the south, lifting the forest away and up from the
waterlogged riverbanks. Even though the slope is by no means sheer, it is still
steep enough that if you lost your footing the possibility of tumbling down the
hill becomes very real. The trees here are mostly conifers, pines and spruces
for the most part, their fallen needles providing a spiky carpet for the forest
floor. To the west a cliff falls sheer into a forested defile.
name: Rising Slope

desc:
The trees form a dense semi-circle in front of you, wildlife in abundance as if
protected by someone or something. They seem to have no fear of anybody and go
about their business totally ignoring your presence. The trees are tall pines,
pools of water scattered about in the gaps between their massive roots. The
trunks of these trees are solid and look as hard as stone, weathered over many
years.
name: Through a Break in the Forest

desc:
Birch trees dominate the forest here, fairly far apart and obstructing movement
little. Westwards the forest climbs a small ridge, which runs for several miles
north and south. About you small pools of water can be seen in small hollows,
where rain water collects running off the ridge. In the east the undergrowth
takes over again, a large section of briars preventing movement there. North
grow the viciously thick brambles native to this area, utterly impassable.
name: Copse of Birches

desc:
Thick patches of brambles sit amongst the trees to the south and west, utterly
impassable unless you have months spare to cut through. Small insects and birds
can be seen flitting from bush to bush. The insects go after the nectar of the
flowers, and the birds go after the insects. To the east a dense grove of pines
stands offering shelter to the animals and plants that live in the forest. North
a great wall of pines almost encloses a large area of grass. A trickle of a
stream runs along the ground and sometimes under it.
name: By a Bramble Patch

desc:
From this point above the trees on a huge outcrop of rock most of the forest is
visible. To the east the Misty Mountains can be seen, their spine arching away
to the north towards the haven of Rivendell. To the south the forest can be seen
stretching to the plains. To the west, beyond the forest, the plains stretch out
towards the horizon. The forest continues in all directions from this point,
with hundreds of yards of brambles preventing access to the east.
name: At a Vantage Point

desc:
A small ridge rises out of the forest here, reaching a peak to the north. The
trees thin towards the top, but in the south the forest resumes its typically
dense nature. A massive solitary oak dominates the west, overhanging the steep
ridge side. Down the ridge to the east a small stand of birch trees provide a
brighter part of the forest. Flowers bloom in the cracks of the rock filling
this area with a wealth of colour.
name: Along a Small Ridge

desc:
The forest rises up a slope to the east here and a large chunk of rock can be
seen jutting out from the forest. The trees surrounding you are mainly oaks and
the ground is littered with acorns. To the west the ground is covered with a
thick layer of briars and thornbushes, while to the north it falls away steeply
into a gloomy defile. Squirrels can be seen scurrying up and down these mighty
trees collecting acorns and carrying on in a way that makes no sense to you.
name: Rising Forest

desc:
The only way to move in these briars is to fight your way through, tearing your
clothing to shreds in the process. The only route that can be taken from here is
to keep heading east to a spot which doesn't look quite so thick. Berries adorn
some of the bushes surrounding you, but getting to them is quite tricky as it
would involve putting your hand into some of the densest thorns.
name: Inside a Briar Hedgerow

desc:
A thick wall of briars and thornbushes prevents heading any further east, so
dense you cannot see more than a yard into it. The trail seems to pick up again
to the south and west on its meandering course through the trees. Wild berries
cover the bushes to the east, although they are unfamiliar. A shiny hard purple
colour, they might not be safe to eat. North the trees hang over the edge of a
deep valley, whose depths are dark.
name: At a Corner in the Forest

desc:
The trail has almost completely disappeared here, leaving only a small gap in
the trees to give its direction. A small rabbit looks up at you inquisitively
before turning tail and darting back into its burrow. Birds can be seen around,
fluttering between nests in the trees above you, their chirping relieving the
eeriness that seems to fill the woods. A tall stand of trees is visible to the
west, high on the horizon.
name: Disappearing Trail

desc:
The trail is almost unrecognizable here as the forest has almost completely
encroached on it. Wild brambles and other shrubs and bushes nestle neatly beside
the trees. To the south impenetrable thorns completely cover the land, while to
the north lies the side of an old wooden cabin. Closer to the trail wild
strawberries grow with prolific gusto in their hundreds. The trail can barely be
made out heading in an east-west direction, overgrown so heavily as it is.
name: Fading Trail

desc:
The trail swings north and east here in a wide sweeping bend. The forest fauna
pauses to look at you as you approach, before darting away into the undergrowth.
To the south the trees take on a strange glossy sheen, from this distance
exactly what is covering them cannot be made out. Moss-covered stumps are the
only sign of man's intervention in this forest, although the fallen trunks have
been removed.
name: Bend in the Trail

desc:
Near the trail the light is far less, the ground beginning to blend into the
trees. Small woodland creatures can be seen darting away from the noise that you
make, disappearing into the gloom. There are so many dead and dried twigs and
branches on the forest floor that even the most skilled of stalkers would have
trouble creeping up on their quarry.
name: Into the Forest

desc:
The trail makes a wide sweeping turn north and east here leading through the
forest. Southwards it would be possible to leave the trail and enter the forest,
but it looks very dark and dense - getting lost seems like a real possibility
should you decide to enter. Birds can be heard singing in the trees around you
but southwards seems devoid of life altogether.
name: Turn in the Trail

desc:
The trail bends south and west here. Set slightly back from it is an old and
dilapidated cabin. The owners of the cabin are nowhere to be seen and it looks
like none have been through here in a while, as many of the tracks left on the
trail are those of animals. Shrubs surround the path towards the door in what
looks like an overgrown garden. Small animals can be heard snuffling about in
the vegetation, but are not visible under the thick bushes.
name: Trail by an Old Cabin

desc:
The inside of the cabin is run-down badly, with holes in the roof and rotted
walls of wood. Around are littered the remnants of this long abandoned abode.
Several bits and pieces lie about giving some clues to the previous owners - a
small wood-burning stove still stands in the corner of the room, and bare
shelves line the walls.
name: Inside an Old Cabin

desc:
Thick roots from the surrounding trees have covered the forest floor here,
causing you to keep stumbling and lose your footing. Strangely the forest is
silent, as there seems to be no animal or plant life here at all apart from
these wandering roots. The trees to the east are different as their leaves have
patches of brown running through them.
name: Wandering Roots

desc:
The trees here are covered in vines and ivy, slowly suffocating the life out of
the forest. On closer inspection there seem to be small animal skeletons trapped
between layers of these vines. The dark green leaves of the ivy almost give the
appearance of leaves on the trees, but is betrayed by the barren or brown
patches where the host tree is visible.
name: Forested Defile

desc:
Tall and barren trees surround you, not encumbered with the ivy or vines of the
nearby woods. The forest floor is still covered with vines and creepers, but all
the trees are dead, their bark stripped away and their life essence sucked out
by these aggressive, marauding creepers. Strange though, there are trees to the
east which look quite healthy.
name: Dead Valley Forest

desc:
The trees seem to loom over you here, a definite threatening presence filling
the valley. There are no vines or bushes here, just some roots stretching off to
the west. These trees are huge and form a wide semi-circle. Out the corner of
your eye you glimpse flickers of movement as if the trees were closing in.
name: Strange Grove in the Valley

desc:
Fallen leaves form a brown mushy carpet here. High above you a thick green
canopy plunges the forest into a green tinted twilight. The trees here are
deciduous oaks and chestnuts, growing fairly close together. Amidst the chaos of
forest life the local fauna carries on, wary of your presence but not perturbed
by it. The forest grows thicker to the south, while to the east an unclimbable
ridge looms.
name: Under a Thick Canopy

desc:
The tall pines to the west and east are sparser here, allowing a whole new flora
to thrive. Small shrubs and bushes clamour for room on the forest floor for the
shafts of available sunlight, while under them many species of animal snuffle
around the earth. Eastwards tall deciduous trees form a thick canopy above you
and these small shrubs and bushes stop abruptly. Westwards a break in the trees
can be seen, possibly indicating a trail of some sort. A huge ring of hewn stone
blocks the exit to the south, while thick impenetrable briars block the route
north.
name: Thinning Pines

desc:
The forest takes on a sharp slant here and the trail has little option but to
follow it. The sharp colour of flowers on bushes and atop thin stems serves to
give a sense of depth to the deepening gloom of the forest on either side of
you. There is a cluster of small shrubs to the west, powerfully scented. The
trail continues north and south of you, disappearing into the forest in both
directions.
name: Slope in the Trail

desc:
Waist high bushes surround you here, many of them adorned with succulent fruit
and berries. To the north there is a break in the forest. Most of the bushes
here are evergreens like holly, covered with bright red berries. From the west a
strong sweet smell of forest flowers wafts in on the wind. Eastwards the forest
drops down a steep bank.
name: Small Bushes

desc:
Small shrubs of varying size are littered about on the forest floor. Some of
them have green leaves, but this is in no way a majority. They vary in size,
shape and colour, almost as many as there are snowflakes on the mountains to the
east. A tall wall of thorns almost secludes this place, as it prevents access to
the north, south, and west. Indeed the only way to leave this place is to return
to the bushes, to the east.
name: Small Shrubs

desc:
The tall trees surrounding you seem to be covered in a sort of slime. It is a
pale green in colour, and seems to be choking the life out of all surrounding
trees. All is quiet here, no small animals nosing around in the undergrowth, no
birds singing in the trees. This area seems as if it has fallen under some evil
curse. The effect is more prominent in the south, while the trail can barely be
seen to the north. An enormous bank of thorns prevents movement east.
name: Sticky Trees

desc:
An abundance of rose bushes fill this small clearing, their fragrance wafting on
the breeze. There are many different colours and varieties, each with a distinct
shape and smell. Butterflies and bees flit from flower to flower, ensuring
nature's cycle and the propagation of the species. The forest looms to the north
and east, while above you to the south and west a rocky ridge side blocks
movement.
name: Amid the Roses

desc:
Trees lie littered around you like fallen warriors, each of them encrusted with
numerous types of fungi and mould. The stench here is overpowering. Any life
that was here would have been consumed by whatever desecrated the forest. Small
pools of foetid water lie around you, as if nature was actively fighting the
cancerous onslaught.
name: Rotting Trees

desc:
This area is in stark contrast to the rest of the forest, where the flora and
fauna thrive. All around you is the musty smell of rotting wood. The trees
themselves have been twisted and deformed, almost as though they have been
frozen whilst writhing in pain. If there could be a visual sign of pain and
suffering, their growth would be it. To the south the twisted branches mingle
completely, forming an impassable wall of bark.
name: Dying Trees

desc:
Tall and mighty conifers surround you here. These trees are so tall that they
seem to touch the clouds in the sky above. Pine cones litter the forest floor
here and squirrels can be seen running along branches. The forest thickens to
the south, but to the east it seems to wither and die. To the north it thickens
completely, making passage in that direction impossible.
name: Tall Conifers

desc:
The sound you make while moving through this area of the forest must be heard
for a long way around. The natural debris littered on the ground and the dry
branches make any silent progress near impossible. Any quarry hunted here will
almost certainly hear your approach. To the west a tall stand of pine trees can
be seen silhouetted against the sky. Eastwards the forest thins slightly, maybe
indicating a track or trail.
name: Noisy Forest

desc:
The forest opens up into a wide glade. To your west the forest resumes its deep
gloom and foreboding air. Eastwards a great ring of stone can be seen, possibly
an ancient temple, but from here it is tricky to make out. The trail follows its
course on a north-south line, winding constantly in the dense woods. Access to
the forest would be possible but hard going, and might entail a round-about
route.
name: Path through the Trees

desc:
Tall pines dominate your view here, the trees growing so close together that the
small spindly branches around trunk level keep snagging on your clothing and
poking you in the eye. Eastwards the grove thins out somewhat and in the west a
tall ring of hewn rock blocks movement. South a dell of trees lies well below
you, inaccessable from here.
name: Deep inside a Cluster of Pines

desc:
The forest begins to thin out here, but to the south the vegetation underneath
them is far too thick to force any sort of entry through. A small ridge rises
off to the north. The forest thins out to the west again but quickly grows very
dense. The forest to the east is moderately dense, but the mix of trees means
that passage is still possible.
name: Grove of Pines

desc:
The forest is very dense here, shadows long and black as pitch in the limited
light. Nothing grows on the forest floor, which is a mish-mash of rotted leaves
and timber. Insects and small animals can be seen clambering over the decay,
gathering food for themselves. To the south the land drops violently, into a
steep depression.
name: Deep within a Forest

desc:
The trail becomes a ford here at the borderline of the forest. The stream
continues from the east to the west. To the north the trail enters a big forest,
while plains dominate the lands south of this ford. An old ruined tower can be
seen just southeast of here, broken down by natural elements and time.
name: Ford at a Forest Trail

desc:
The trail continues south and north through the forest. Many animal tracks lead
off to the east into the trees, disappearing into thick undergrowth. Some of
them are quite large, possibly a bear or bigger, but most are the small tracks
of foxes and the like. Mountain peaks jut out above the forest far to the east,
their snow-capped peaks dipping in and out of the clouds.
name: Forest Trail

desc:
The trail takes a sharp incline here and the forest follows. Large pine trees
dominate the skyline to the east and the trail seems to follow them. Birds can
be heard singing in the shrubs and small trees to the north, but none can be
seen. Delicate flowers are visible among the grasses and bushes that line the
trail.
name: Rising Path through the Forest

desc:
Just off the trail here, the immensity of this forest is clear. It seems to
stretch for leagues around with no end in sight. To the east the distant peaks
of the Misty Mountains can be made out against the sky, at night blacking out
the stars and by day hanging over the surrounding plains like ominous watching
giants.
name: Off the Trail

desc:
The bushes surrounding you all have the same sickly yellow hue. On closer
examination it is obvious the disease has entered these shrubs by the roots. and
worked its way to every leaf and shoot, slowly killing as it went. Even the
light here has a gangrenous hue, almost as if the poison in the ground has
infected it too. South the twisted undergrowth grows very dense, utterly
impassable without an army of axe-wielders, while east it lessens. It might be
possible to clear away the dead sticks there, and leave this poisoned part of
the forest.
name: Sickly Bushes

desc:
Small shrubs seem to dance all around, their flowers and branches waving in the
slight breeze. There is something strange about the smell here - it reeks of
decay, and seems to be coming from the west. Even the fragrance of the flowers
is not enough to block it out. Small animals can be seen darting between bushes,
disappearing into the densely twisted vegetation to the north.
name: Among Flowering Shrubs

desc:
The trail bends to the south and east here. Small shrubs line the trail on both
sides, and a few animal tracks can be seen. A cluster of shrubs off the path to
the west provides a relief of colour, compared to the constant greenery of the
surrounding vegetation. Dense forest prohibits any progress to the north, as the
growth is so tightly packed there is no way to get through.
name: Corner of the Trail

desc:
The trail passes through a dense patch of conifers, their brown straggly twigs
brushing against your arms and face. Looking above the canopy you see a thick
mass of dark green needles, letting no more than a minimal amount of light fall
to ground. Rustlings can be heard either side of you, but the forest is much too
thick to see what makes them. Small shrubs give off a pleasant scent to the
south, where the vegetation grows less dense.
name: Closely Wooded Trail

desc:
The trail makes a sweeping bend north and west here. To the west the forest
thickens considerably and comes right down to the trail. Tiny flowers can be
seen poking their heads through the grass and small shrubs that line the trail,
their smell quite noticeable in air. A tall stand of trees can be seen to the
east, with patches of brown in the dark green canopy.
name: Bend in the Trail

desc:
The trees surrounding you have been mauled here, all stripped of their bark and
left to die. The score marks of knives and axes can be seen in the trunks, the
marks of man not beast. In the south a tall palisade can be seen rising out of
the trees, the trail to the west of it. In the east a mass of fallen trunks and
tangled growth prevents further movement in that direction.
name: Battered Trees

desc:
The forest drops into a small dell here, the trees growing so closely together
that they wall you in on all sides. The ground underfoot is soft and spongy.
with small puddles of water nestled between the roots of the great trees. Tiny,
delicate flowers are dotted all around, a carpet of colour in this isolated
spot.
name: Forest End

desc:
The rolling foothills of the mountains to the east are covered in trees, mostly
hardy conifers. There are a few small deciduous trees dotted about, in small
groves like islands amongst the pines. Small creatures can be heard snuffling
about between the trees constantly, often hidden by the dense growth. There are
some small birds flitting from tree to tree, oblivious to your presence and
carrying on with their daily life. The hills continue to the east and south,
while the forest thickens and becomes impassable west and north.
name: Forested Hills

desc:
High cliffs surround this hill on three sides, leaving west as the only way to
leave. The trees themselves are very sparse and the forest becomes disjointed
here. In the quiet of the forest howls and growls can be heard ringing around
the hills in the distance. Some of the trees have huge gouges in their bark, as
if they had been mauled by a large animal.
name: Tree Covered Hills

desc:
Scree and shingle dominate the plains here, providing very little grazing for
the animals that usually inhabit the plains. The ground is very hard, with only
a thin layer of topsoil. A consequence of this is the very small plants that can
grow here, making the plains themselves very bleak. East the ground rises up
onto a sharp ridge, some way above the forest.
name: High Plains

desc:
The forest begins to climb rapidly to the east, the trees mainly small conifers
with large spaces between them. The trampled undergrowth below them suggests
that this part of the forest is frequented by some heavy herd of animals. To the
north there are large tree-covered hills, while to the east the forest gives way
to vast plains.
name: Climbing in the Forest

desc:
Many of the trees here have been cut down recently, their thick trunks still
lain on the earth. Some have been dragged off, with the tracks in the earth
leading west. Off to the east the forest begins to climb up into the mountains
that dominate the skyline, while northwards the forest heads into the twilight
made by the thick canopy of leaves.
name: Palisade in the Trees

desc:
A large wooden gate stands before you, made from the massive hardwood trunks of
the forest around this area. The ground here has been ripped and trampled into a
thick mud from the constant traffic in and out of the camp. It clings and sucks
at anything that walks into it, making walking all the more tiresome. The gate
forms a break in a tall palisade, extending east and west.
name: In Front of a Gate

desc:
Moving through the briars and bushes that adorn the side of the trail is very
tiring and energy sapping. Small flowers grow about your feet and colourful
berries cling to the surrounding bushes as they sway in the breeze, mingling
freely in the briar patches. To the south-east a tall palisade rises between the
trees, probably a settlement of sorts. The wall runs from east to west, over ten
feet high.
name: Beside the Trail

desc:
Small shrubs, maybe six inches high, litter the forest floor with a riotous
cacophony of colour and fragrance. The smell alone is potent enough, but with
the countless colours it forms an extremely distinctive brew. East and south a
hedge of briars block movement, as impassable and strong as brick wall.
name: Small Shrubs by the Trail

desc:
Staggered trees force the trail to meander in and out between them. These trees
are huge, at least six feet in diameter. Underneath their prodigious boughs it
seems as if they are standing as tall as the clouds themselves - even the Misty
Mountains to the east cannot be seen due to their height. They lead off into the
horizon in all directions, a sea of trees without any visible landmarks.
name: Meandering Trail through the Trees

desc:
The trail meanders through the huge trees, each one with a trunk of immense
girth. Small birds can be heard squawking and chirping high in the canopy above
you, but it is dense enough they cannot be seen. Small woodland critters can
also be heard snuffling around in the undergrowth. Countless many flowers and
weeds decorate the forest floor on either side of you, growing onto the path in
many places.
name: On a Meandering Trail

desc:
The trail winds in and about the trees here and it is all to easy to lose it in
the undergrowth. Small forest animals scurry across the trail in front and
behind you, ignoring your presence. The trees look ancient and have an old
gnarled appearance to them, towering over the path. Small shrubs and vines
scramble for any light that gets through that overhanging natural wall.
name: On a Meandering Trail

desc:
Animal skeletons litter the floor of the den. In one corner a pile of droppings
fills whole area with its pungent odour. The bushes surrounding this clearing
are broken, their mangled branches forming a carpet on the forest floor. The
trees surrounding this spot form a secluded area in the forest, making it safe
for the mother bear to leave her cubs when she ventures out to hunt.
name: Bear Den

desc:
Between these large and battered bushes an open clearing in the undergrowth has
been made, the vegetation seemingly crushed by the passage of some large animal
footprints. There are no berries or flowers on the bushes, only the the town
branches where they once grew. Large padded footprints lead towards the bushes
to the west. To the north and east a barrier of completely tangled bushes and
vines block all movement.
name: Forest Bushes

desc:
A high palisade of wood rises up before you. The palisade made from the stout
and strong forest trees rises up about twenty-five feet to be topped with large
wooden spikes making it totally unclimbable. The forest continues on to the west
and some smaller bushes can be seen to the north. Beyond the palisade to the
east and south the sound of animals can be heard along with the occasional
voice.
name: Along a High Palisade

desc:
A tall wall of wooden stakes rises out of the ground to the west. The tops of
have been sharpened for added defense, making the wall unscalable. An animal
path lies to the south, cut out of the thick forest vegetation by massive paws
and constant movement. The local forest creatures take no notice of this wall,
scurrying around right up at its base.
name: Beside a Large Wooden Wall

desc:
The ground next to the pool of water in front of you has been trampled into a
thick mud by the repeated visits of forest animals. Its proximity to the nearby
plains means that the grazing animals that live there also use this place to
drink. Predators too must frequent this spot, for trails of blood lie streaked
on the ground.
name: Watering Hole

desc:
A sharp ridge rises out of the plains and heads east, climbing steadily up into
the mountains beyond. Small mountain blooms litter the grass about your feet, a
burst of colour in the bleak hillside plateau. A couple of shrubs sit lonely by
themselves here and there, breaking up the otherwise unchanging height of the
swaying grass.
name: Ridge in the Plains

desc:
Vast plains of grass open up before you, climbing towards the east where the
Misty Mountains sit overlooking this country. A ridge climbs above the plains to
the north, with a steep drop to the forest, while south the forest begins again.
In places around here the grass has been cropped short by the grazing of
animals, heavy beasts judging by the pulped earth in parts.
name: Grazing Plains

desc:
In the foothills of the mountains here, the trees are sparser than in the main
forest. Most of the trees are conifers, stunted by the altitude. A flat and
barren plateau lies to the east, before the snow-tipped peaks which dominate the
skyline and landscape for miles around.
name: Elevated Forestland

desc:
Birds can be heard chirping in the trees above you, while many small animals are
visible all around. This is an especially well populated area of the woods,
perhaps because of the soil's well-watered fertility. Large, wide trees grow
close together to the north, while to the south the land begins to be rather
water-logged.
name: Stream of Tracks

desc:
This is obviously a path frequented by large game, judging by the four legged
footprints that litter it. The tracks head east and west, while to the north a
large wall of sharp wooden stakes looms out of the forest. A small track leads
round its outer base, northeast of here, and out of your sight.
name: Animal Path

desc:
Large footprints weave in an out of the trees, following the hoof tracks of the
herbivores that live in the forest. Underfoot crushed flowers and plants lie
everywhere. The trees here are entirely deciduous - oaks and birches. Their
fallen leaves litter the ground below, adding a soft layer to the earth.
name: Faintly-Wooded Land

desc:
Sparsely growing trees stand in isolated groves here, not impeding movement
much. To the north there is a mass of bushes, many bruised and bashed by the
animals that continually pad through them. The trees themselves are mainly
deciduous oaks and birches, an almost watertight roof overhead. The forest floor
itself still seems to be rather muddy though, perhaps due to the river nearby to
the south.
name: Light Forest

desc:
Masses of animal tracks seem to congregate here, making any attempt at tracking
a particular animal impossible. Even your own footprints seem to be lost in the
miasma that lies before you. Some of the larger tracks disappear in the bushes
to the north, perhaps where some hidden lair lies. No flowers grow here, all
have been long trampled before they could grow to any size.
name: Cluster of Tracks

desc:
Hundreds of animal tracks criss-cross in front of you, darting in front and
behind trees. The tracks are a mish-mash of various animals, from the hooves of
deer to the big pads of bears. In the north thick bushes prevent block all
movement, unless you wish to spend weeks cutting through the undergrowth.
name: Tracks in the Trees

desc:
The stream widens at this point, forcing the water to flow slowly westwards. It
seems like the depth of the water grows shallow in the west direction but it is
still deep enough to allow any small craft to flow through. The banks of the
stream are quite steep at most places but several small beaches make it possible
to climb up the bank easily. An old tower is visible through the trees on the
south bank.
name: Stream along the Forest

desc:
As the stream bends westward it seems to get wider and deeper. The north-eastern
bank seem flat and easy to climb, but the south-western bank looks much steeper
and is covered with thorny bushes in most places. Some large trees can be
spotted towards north and east, and on the south-western side of the stream an
old tower can been seen through the vegetation.
name: Bend in the Forest Stream

desc:
The forest comes to an end to the south and west, where a small stream cuts
through the landscape. Even though a few large trees can be seen, the forest is
made up primarily of small, thin trees and dense bushes. It appears that the
forest gets more dense towards the northeast.
name: Light Forest near a Stream

desc:
A bushy tangle of vegetation grows thick in the outskirts of a light forest
which lies to both the east and the west. Young saplings and grass thrive with
abandon and cover the ground in a confusion of growth and foliage. A stream
flows to the south and it looks as though a number of tracks from the plentiful
wildlife in this area can be seen to the north.
name: Outskirts of the Forest

desc:
A healthy mixture of smaller trees and undergrowth make up this wooded forest,
giving some protection against the elements and large predators that roam the
area, looking for smaller prey. Soft, green grass and a few stray branches cover
the floor of the forest and the animal path to the north suggest that wildlife
is abundant in this area. The forest begins to grow boggy to the east.
name: Light Forest

desc:
Water spilling over the stream bank to the south makes this part of the forest
almost impassable even on foot. The ground is muddy and boggy to the point that
a traveller should be careful not to linger too long in one spot, for fear of
being stuck permanently. The trees here are very healthy and excessively tall,
due to the abundant supply of water. Dark green moss covers the trunks of the
trees and a good portion of the ground as well. All the moisture in the area
probably makes for a good breeding ground for small insects.
name: Watery Forest

desc:
All around the trees grow dense and thick in this area of the forest, their
trunks wide in girth and their branches rising proudly to the sky. There are
signs everywhere that this forest is thick with small game and the vegetation is
flourishing green and healthy. The trees seem to thin out to the south and a
path is visible to the north. Towards the west, the ground grows decidedly more
boggy.
name: Rising Forest

desc:
Both plains vegetation and young seedlings fight to exist here in the outskirts
of the forest rising up to the west. Tall grains and short grasses flourish
alongside the half-grown trees, creating a wild mixture of foliage and ground
cover. One would imagine small game would especially favour this area, using the
woods as protection against the larger predators and the grains and seeds
providing ample food.
name: Outskirts of the Forest

desc:
The vegetation is varied on the outskirts of this forest, but it is obvious that
the forest is winning over the plains grass in the competition for survival and
will soon take over completely. A stream can be seen running to the south and a
lightly wooded forest begins to the west. High plains rise up directly to your
east.
name: Outskirts of the Forest

desc:
Various types of wildlife abound in this lightly wooded portion of the land.
Deer especially seem to favor this spot, perhaps drawn by the protection offered
by the forest from predators that inhabit the nearby plains. The trees grow
straight and tall, nourished by the rich, fertile soil and plentiful supply of
water. A small stream runs to the west and south of the forest, but it seems the
bank is too steep in either direction to enter it.
name: Light Forest Bordering a Stream

desc:
Winding through a quiet forest, the stream bends here from the south to the
west. It appears that the north bank of the stream floods frequently,
overflowing into the forest in that direction. The light forest to the east is
probably home to some small game but for now you might want to just follow the
stream where it chooses to lead you.
name: Streambend next to the Forest

desc:
This stream flows quiet and gentle next to the lightly wooded forest bordering
it to the north. Layers of think, green moss trail down the side of the bank and
the occasional water lily floats delicately along the edge of the stream. The
stream floats from the east to the west and a path is visible to the south.
name: Close to a Streambend

desc:
Bordering the footpath that raises to the south, the stream here is very wide
and the current strong, flowing from the east to the west with speed and
fervour. The outskirts of a forest are visible to the north.
name: Stream Bordering a Footpath

desc:
The water flows gently westwards through the almost flat landscape. On the south
bank of the stream a small trail can be seen following the stream closely. The
banks of the stream are quite low and would make it easy to leave the stream in
that direction.
name: Stream Following a Trail

desc:
Two small streams coming from the south and east join together and form a bigger
stream flowing northwards. Where the two streams meet the water gets turbulent,
forming small waves and eddies. The western bank is quite steep and covered with
many small bushes that make progress in that direction quite impossible. At the
crest of the western bank the top of an old tower can be seen rising over the
bushes.
name: A Crossing of Streams

desc:
Passing a ruined tower the trail passes a stream to the north, and to the south
the trail continues among the plains. Further north you can see a big forest
branching out over the plains. The tower just east of you is worn down by time,
wind and perhaps even battles. On this western side of the tower there are some
windows, but no entrance can be seen this way.
name: Trail by a Ruined Tower

desc:
Following alongside a footpath that lies to the south, the stream travels from
the east to the west. The water is very clear here and is full of tiny fish and
other small creatures. A forest rising to the north casts a gloomy shadow in
that direction.
name: Stream Following a Footpath

desc:
The current of the stream picks up speed here, rushing over small rocks and
branches in its way. The bank to the south seems to be decaying from the
constant wear of the water but the bank to the north is still quite solid. A
light forest borders the stream in that direction.
name: Flowing Stream

desc:
The stream makes a lazy bend here, flowing from the east to the north. A tilted
footpath can be seen to the south, runnning parallel to the stream. The gentle
flow of the current and quiet sound of the water combine to make a traveller
wonder if it might not be better to stay among these hills and follow the stream
wherever it may lead.
name: Turn in the Stream

desc:
This flat, dirt footpath makes its way through the countryside, allowing for
easy travel through the plains and hills. It is very narrow and large groups
would be most comfortable travelling single file along the path. A stream
borders the path to the north and east and the path itself bends here,
continuing on both to the south and west.
name: Bend in the Path

desc:
The footpath comes across a small stream flowing slowly northwards. The stream
is quite shallow and the sandy bottom makes crossing easy - even the shortest of
hobbits would not get his ears wet crossing this stream. The banks of the stream
are covered with bushes and small trees. While the land north of here looks
fairly flat, a slight rise in the landscape is clearly visible towards the
southeast.
name: Ford at a Stream Crossing

desc:
A quiet stream makes its way through the land, flowing from the south and
crossing a footpath at the ford to the north. A portion of grainy lowlands is
visible to the east and a trails cuts a brown swath through the land to the
west.
name: Gentle Stream

desc:
Travelling parallel to the footpath to the west, this gentle stream runs from
the south to the north in direction through a series of hills. A watery portion
of elevated land rises to the east, creating a bank which looks to be too steep
and slippery to climb from this side.
name: Stream among the Hills

desc:
The gentle stream bends from the east to the north, running its slow course
through the plains and nourishing both land and animals with its abundant supply
of water. It continues on north through a range of hills and runs close to the
ford that is visible to the east. Although, as you examine the banks of the
stream, it is clear that it would be just as easy to leave the stream at this
point and step onto the trail that lies directly to the west.
name: Bend in the Stream

desc:
Quiet and soft, the gentle current of the stream flows westwards through the
plains. A ford to the south gives one the opportunity to step onto a trail which
appears to run east-west. Uneven plains cut away to the east or you can climb up
onto a watery elevation rising up to the north.
name: Close to a Ford

desc:
A broken down tower looms just to the north, while the trail itself forms a
t-crossing. Westwards it turns towards the forest that lies to the north, south
it follows a stream while to the east there is a ford allowing you to safely
pass the stream and continue on.
name: Crossing in the Trail

desc:
Running side by side with the stream to the east, this wide trail continues on
straight to both the north and south. A junction of this trail and another is
directly to the north and you can spy flat grasslands opening up to the east. A
tower is visible not far in the distance to the north.
name: Along a Stale Trail

desc:
Wide and well-beaten down, this trail makes travel through the plains and hills
easy whether one is on foot or is mounted. It leads both north and south for
some distance, running between the stream to the east and the open grasslands to
the west.
name: Along a Stale Trail

desc:
The well worn trail cuts a brown swath through the grasslands, extending north
and south for several miles in direction. A small stream bends away from the
trail to the east and very far to the north, one can see the top of a tower
rising up high above the plains.
name: Along a Stale Trail

desc:
This trail is evidently quite popular among travellers, judging by how firmly
the earth is packed down underneath you from countless feet and hooves. A small
campsite appears to have been constructed to the south for those weary from the
journey. The trail begins to bend, travelling both north and east in direction.
name: Bend in the Stale Trail

desc:
Two trails cross here, one running east and west and the other extending
straight south as far as the eye can see. A shallow place to ford the stream is
visible to the east and a large stream runs to the north.
name: A Crossing of Trails

desc:
The stone walls of this circular building are cold and most items are covered
with dust and debris. A wooden staircase follows along the wall leading upwards
towards the roof where an opening can be seen. The floor has marks in the dust
from the feet of minor animals and in some places human footprints can also be
seen. Other than a few broken chairs lying on the floor, most of the furniture
left is still in pretty decent shape.
name: Ruined Tower

desc:
The top of this tower appears to be stable, despite obvious damage caused by the
wear of wind and rain upon the wood and stone. The scars of battles long past
show in the broken battlements that line the top of the tower and the gaping
holes visible in several places along the tower floor. To the north, one can see
a large forest stretching itself along a stream, and plains continue to the west
and south. Eastwards the forest continues alongside a trail, and even further
east you can see the Misty Mountains.
name: Top of the Ruined Tower

desc:
This dark and dusty cellar has little life left except for a few small rats
hiding among the rubble. The wine racks lie empty and broken bottles lie on the
floor in testimony to the wear of time and neglect upon this tower. The air is
rather damp and it is hard to see clearly but it almost seems as though the
shadows move behind you as you turn to head back up the stairs.
name: Dusty Cellar

desc:
This trail slowly bends north and east from this point, but as you look east you
see that the trail continues south and east. South of here the plains unfolds
into a great flat landscape. Looking north you see a ruined tower to the
northeast, and behind it a stream flanks a big forest.
name: Bend in the Trail

desc:
A large expanse of level grasslands begins here and spreads south as far as the
eye can see. The land itself is thick and green with a short, lush grass and a
few wildflowers dot the landscape with their bright colors. A trail can be seen
to both the north and east and there is a stream close by a little ways to the
east.
name: Flat Grassland

desc:
Flat and monotonous, these grasslands continue to the north and south and a
broad trail runs directly to the east. The ground is covered primarily by a
short, bright green grass but one can see the occasional wildflower or patch of
taller grass flourishing brightly.
name: Flat Grassland

desc:
These level grasslands expand lush and green to both the north and south for
some distance. The soil is evidently quite fertile and both short and tall wild
grasses take advantage of the good conditions, growing thick and abundantly.
Looking to the east, you see a broad trail.
name: Flat Grassland

desc:
A thick carpet of lush grass covers the ground in this area of the land.
Occasionally you might catch a glimpse of a rabbit hiding in the tall grass or
larger animals grazing off the healthy vegetation. The flat grasslands extend
both north and south and a trail runs directly to the east.
name: Flat Grassland

desc:
A small footpath runs straight and narrow between a stream to the north and the
grainy lowlands that lie directly to the south. It fords a small stream to the
west and continues on to the east as well. The air is very fresh and clear and
the dirt of the footpath below is level and smooth.
name: Footpath near a Ford

desc:
Built at a steep angle into the land, this footpath is elevated enough to run
slightly above the stream to the north and the lowlands that are thick with
grain to the south. Looking farther north, you can see a forest rising up in
that direction. The footpath continues straight on both east and west.
name: Elevated Footpath

desc:
Tall grains thrive in this low, moist portion of land. A few tufts of grass are
visible among the thick, brown grains, flourishing in the fertile soil and a
plentiful supply of the water from the stream to the west. A footpath can be
seen to the north and to the south, a steep bank leads up to an elevated piece
of land.
name: Grainy Lowland

desc:
A wild variety of brown and gold grains sprout up across the lowlands here and
to the west. A footpath borders the lowlands to the north and looking south, you
can see the beginnings of a jagged range of hills.
name: Grainy Lowland

desc:
Jagged hills to the south and fields of grain shining gold to the west border
this footpath which runs from the east and makes its way north. The path has a
pronounced slope which makes walking or riding somewhat difficult.
name: Sloping Curve in the Footpath

desc:
The natural erosion of the land has caused this portion of the footpath to tilt
from the north to the south, making travel on any kind of mount impossible and
even travel on foot is somewhat hindered. The footpath continues on, running
west-east in direction.
name: Tilting Footpath

desc:
Numerous potholes make travelling on the path here difficult. You resort to
picking your way around them carefully and can only guess the condition of the
path deteriorates due to flooding from the stream to the north. This section of
the path lies in a low area of the land and it is probably under water during
the rainier seasons of the year.
name: Decaying Footpath

desc:
Probably a hunting path at one time, constant travel has made this an actual
path which follows the stream to the north closely. Still, patches of grass and
weeds can be seen sprouting up, threatening to take over the path and return it
to wild grass again. A flat area of grass is thriving to the south and you can
enter the stream flowing to the north of the path. The path itself continues on,
running straight west-east.
name: Footpath close to the Stream

desc:
Blanketed by an abundance of taller grass and flowers, this flatland seems to be
a popular grazing area for all types of animals. Paths corner the land to the
north and east and grassy hills are visible to the south.
name: Grassy Flatland

desc:
Barely hills at all, the soft, subtle inclines of this hill range are covered
with a short, green grass. Travel through these hills is by no means difficult
and the crest of the hills merely gives a traveller the chance to survey the
lands that surround. The hills flatten out into a grassy level area of land to
the north and a path is visible to both the east and the south. The hills
themselves continue on to the west.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
This small, level path runs straight to both the east and west, providing and
easy way to make one's journey through the plains and hills that dominate this
land to the north and south. It appears that another trail meets this one to the
east and looking further south-east, one can see a settlement has been
established.
name: Path Close to a Crossing

desc:
A gently sloping path runs from the east to the west and is bordered by the
brook to the south and the hills that begin to the north. The angle of the path,
although not quite even, is barely enough to notice and allows one to
concentrate on the journey ahead.
name: Sloping Path among the Hills

desc:
Judging by the numerous hoofprints and footpints on this path, it is frequented
quite often, probably by the settlers in this area wishing to cross the stream
at the ford some distance to the west. Plains stretch out to the north and the
path itself continues on to the east and west.
name: Bridle Path

desc:
A makeshift bridle path runs from the settlement that is visible to the far east
all the way to the ford that crosses the stream a little ways to the west. The
path is rather wide and well beaten down from all the travel of men and horses.
Jagged plains stretch out to the north and a small rivulet trickles through the
hills to the south.
name: Bumpy Bridle Path

desc:
This path was probably established as a way to lead the horses to the ford from
the settlement visible further to the east. Numerous footprints are apparent all
over the ground and one can imagine that it would be easy to ford the stream to
the west on foot or mounted. Plains extend to the north and a stream flows
swiftly to the south.
name: Bridle Path next to a Ford

desc:
The water is just low enough here to allow travellers to ford the stream running
from the south to the north and continue on the trail east and west. The bottom
of the stream is very soft and mushy and small stones and pebbles crunch
underneath as you cross. Two streams merge to the south and this trail joins
another to the west.
name: Ford at a Trail Crossing

desc:
A series of gentle hills rise softly and continue on east. Bright wildflowers
and green grass cover the hills and help keep the land firm and soft to travel
upon. A grassy scarp rises up to the north and a path makes its way through the
hills to the south.
name: Grassy Hills

desc:
A high, sharp uprising of rock forms a scarp facing the edge of the hills to the
south. It is covered with a soft, green grass and gives a good view of the
surrounding lands. A footpath is directly north and the land levels out to a
grassy flatland to the east.
name: Grassy Scarp

desc:
A sharp outcropping of earth and rock creates a jagged ridge which turns into a
grassy scarp to the east. The footpath to the north tilts in this direction and
a range of hills rises up to the west. Looking south, you can see grass covered
plains begin to stretch out all the way to the horizon in a sea of green and
brown grasses.
name: Jagged Ridge

desc:
Rough plains covered with a short brownish grass cut a jagged edge between the
hills to the east and the ridge to the north. The plains continue to the west
and a bridle path runs east-west to the south. Not far in the distance, there is
a large settlement visible to the south-east.
name: Grassy Jagged Plains

desc:
Cutting their way into the hills, these jagged plains extend to the east and
west with an expanse of brownish grass. One would guess that these plains do not
get much water as they are very dry and the grass is very poor due to constant
exposure to the unfriendly combination of wind, lack of rain, and poor soil. A
bridle path runs to the south and a hill range begins to the north.
name: Jagged Plains

desc:
Travel through the range of jagged hills that rise up before you is difficult
but not impossible. The hills themselves continue on to the east but seem to
flatten out into plains to the south. An area of grainy lowlands is visible to
the north and a watery elevation of land can be entered to the west.
name: Jagged Hills

desc:
These jagged plains slope sharply into small crests that could almost be termed
hills if they were any steeper. A soft, thick grass covers the ground, mixed
with a few stray grains. A hill range looms to the north and a bridle path is
visible to the south. One could enter the stream directly west.
name: Uneven Plains

desc:
An elevated piece of land allows a clear view of the surrounding areas,
revealing a series of low, jagged hills to the east and a section of grainy
lowlands which lie to the north. Due to a natural spring bubbling up cool, clear
water continuously, the ground in this area is quite soggy and water is standing
in some areas. During the spring and summer months, there is probably a good
deal of mud to contend with.
name: Watery Elevation

desc:
A jagged set of hills rises up here and to the west. Loose stones and unstable
rocks makes travelling through them somewhat treacherous but not impossible. One
is forced to choose a path through them with care. Plains back up to the hills
to the south and a footpath curves away to the north.
name: Jagged Hills

desc:
This building seems to be more important than the others. There is only one bed
in the corner and shelves on the wall have various jars and other implements on
them. Against the western wall a small fireplace sits providing some warmth in
the bitter winters that are commonplace in this country. On the south wall hangs
a large tapestry depicting the life of the foresters, and the settlements they
hail from.
name: Inside the Chief's Hut

desc:
You have entered a small encampment surrounded by a tall wooden palisade. A fire
dominates the centre of the camp with a couple of huts on either side. The huts
themselves are old but have been made with some craftsmanship from the
surrounding forest. To the north a large, reinforced wooden gate stands and
looks like it would keep out the most determined hordes. Around you people go
about their business always keeping one eye on you, the strangers.
name: In a Small Encampment

desc:
Upon entering this austere hut, there seems to be very little in the way of
creature comfort. Bunks line the northern and southern walls. Another door in
the eastern wall leads back outside. The bunks themselves are made of plain
planks. A thin blanket on each, which are so thin, they can only supply very
little protection and almost none during the winter months. A few possessions
are littered around and the only light and a little warmth is provided by a lam
on a small and simple table.
name: Inside a Common Hut

desc:
Many mounts mill around in this paddock. Here the beasts are kept, used by the
loggers to drag the huge trees they fall to the camp, where they can be cut and
then moved on to the Dunlending villages further south. Apart from the animals,
watering and feeding troughs, also here is a small area used for storing a few
of the great trunks.
name: In a Paddock

desc:
The bumpy worn trail continues onto a bizarre natural bridge over the fissure, a
slice of rock twenty foot wide. Below, the fissure drops away into depths too
dark to perceive. Billowing grass with many small colourful flowers cover the
rock here, a natural garden suspended on high.
name: A Natural Bridge over a Fissure

desc:
You stand inside a large, megalithic, stone structure. These huge stones are
arranged in a sequence of rectangular pillars rising in height on each side of
the circle to a climax in the east. The stones have none of the characteristics
of the local rock and must have been hauled here in ages past from some distant
quarry. In the centre is a large stone slab with a huge number of notches and
scores - looking closer it seems to be stained red in places.
name: Circle of Stones
